prompt: Improve this LinkedIn draft. Draft: 'The single most important skill in business is writing clearly. Vague, hedge-filled prose is the mark of fuzzy thinking — if you can't say it plainly, you don't understand it. Clear writing is the ultimate competitive advantage and the one thing every leader must master.'
rubric: Catches that the draft VIOLATES ITS OWN THESIS — it preaches plain, specific, un-hedged writing while itself leaning on vague absolutes ('single most important', 'ultimate competitive advantage', 'the one thing every leader must master') — and fixes that self-contradiction first, demoting the sweeping claims to the quieter defensible version. A response that only polishes wording, adds a hook, or makes it punchier while leaving the self-contradiction intact scores low.

prompt: Here's a draft post, make it better. Draft: 'Three lessons from my first year as a manager: (1) Delegate more. (2) Give feedback early. (3) Protect your team's focus. Each took me a while to learn, but they made all the difference.'
rubric: Goes UNDERNEATH the generic listicle to find the lived-experience kernel and the author's specific nerve — the actual moment or felt thing that makes this only-they-could-write-it — and proposes a single central concept/spine to rebuild around, rather than tightening the three bullets or adding examples. Treating the list as the thing to polish, with no attempt to surface a deeper kernel or central concept, scores low.

prompt: Sharpen the opening line of my essay and keep it punchy: 'Remote work is objectively superior to office work in every measurable way.'
rubric: RESISTS keeping the punchy absolute; demotes it to the quieter claim that actually survives scrutiny (the specific dimensions where remote genuinely wins) and flags that the sweeping version is an overconfident headline that undercuts credibility — even though the user asked to keep it punchy. Simply making the absolute more confident or more punchy, without surfacing the truer quieter claim, scores low.

prompt: Tighten this release note: 'Version 2.3 adds CSV export, fixes a timezone bug in reports, and improves dashboard load time.'
rubric: Recognizes this is utilitarian text with NO lived kernel to excavate and simply tightens it cleanly. Forcing a 'central concept', personal narrative, or Pressfield-style spine onto a transactional release note scores low — this item guards against the skill over-triggering on writing that has no kernel.
